coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of fluorescent tartaric acid-modified polymer dots for Fe3+ detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhonghao
Li, Dan
Du, Wenting
Du, Yinuo
Yang, Guang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, fluorescent polymer dots (T-PDs) with excellent properties were synthesized through a facile magnetic stirring method using carboxymethyl cellulose derivative (CMC-NH2) and tartaric acid (TA). The synthesized T-PDs exhibit a nanoscale structure, excellent stability and good fluorescence behavior with a fluorescence quantum yield of 12.5 %. Moreover, the T-PDs can be selected for the design of sensors for detecting Fe3+ via the internal filtration effect (IFE) through a fluorescence quenching process. A good linear equation can be fitted between (F0-F/F0) and the amount of Fe3+ from 0 to 600 μM (R2 = 0.998) and the detection limit (LOD) for Fe3+ was determined to be 0.74 μM (3σ/slope). Additionally, the as-prepared sensing platform can be utilized for detecting Fe3+ in real samples, which indicates its great potential for application in sensing. Interestingly, the fluorescence of the quenched system can be partially recovered by the addition L-cysteine. Thus, a fluorescent “ON-OFF-ON” switch can be designed, and a resettable and multireadout logic gate operation can be realized.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer dots
Fluorescent sensor
Carboxymethyl cellulose
Fe3+ detection
Logic gate operation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation of tartaric acid-functionalized polymer dots (T-PDs)
Fluorescence analytical method for Fe3+ detection
Fe3+ detection based on the T-PDs
The fabrication of configurable logic gate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image and (b) particle size distribution of T-PDs.
(a) The XPS spectrum, and high-resolution spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s of the T-PDs.

(a) The UV–vis spectrum, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of T-PDs (inset photos of the T-PDs solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm light irradiation (right), (b) fluorescence emission spectra of T-PDs with different excitation wavelengths.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of the T-PDs with the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ from 0 ∼ 600 μM, (b) linear relationship between the (F0-F)/F0 and the Fe3+ concentration ranging from 60 ∼ 600 μM, λexi: 390 nm.

(a) Fluorescence lifetime of the T-PDs before and after adding different concentrations of Fe3+, and (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3+, fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of T-PDs.
Truth table of the INHIBIT logic gate operation for the T-PDs.
Schematic showing the synthetic route and application for T-PDs.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of fluorescent tartaric acid-modified polymer dots for Fe3+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Hexing Road 26, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Hexing Road 26, Harbin 150040, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study described a facile method for the preparation of fluorescent polymer dots (T-PDs) via a simple magnetic stirring method using carboxymethyl cellulose derivative (CMC-NH2) and tartaric acid (TA). The T-PDs exhibited bright fluorescence emission and can be utilized for fluorescent detecting Fe3+ by internal filtration effect (IFE) mechanism. The T-PDs based sensor can be used for tracing Fe3+ in real water samples with reliability and practicality. Additionally, a configurable logic gate operation of INHIBIT was achieved successfully.

Herein, fluorescent polymer dots (T-PDs) with excellent properties were synthesized through a facile magnetic stirring method using carboxymethyl cellulose derivative (CMC-NH2) and tartaric acid (TA). The synthesized T-PDs exhibit a nanoscale structure, excellent stability and good fluorescence behavior with a fluorescence quantum yield of 12.5 %. Moreover, the T-PDs can be selected for the design of sensors for detecting Fe3+ via the internal filtration effect (IFE) through a fluorescence quenching process. A good linear equation can be fitted between (F0-F/F0) and the amount of Fe3+ from 0 to 600 μM (R2 = 0.998) and the detection limit (LOD) for Fe3+ was determined to be 0.74 μM (3σ/slope). Additionally, the as-prepared sensing platform can be utilized for detecting Fe3+ in real samples, which indicates its great potential for application in sensing. Interestingly, the fluorescence of the quenched system can be partially recovered by the addition L-cysteine. Thus, a fluorescent “ON-OFF-ON” switch can be designed, and a resettable and multireadout logic gate operation can be realized.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets used or analyzed in this work are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: With rapid technological and economic development, water pollution has become a major environmental issue. Heavy metals that exist in waste water accumulate continuously in organisms through breathing or food, thus causing serious diseases [1,2]. Among them, Fe3+ acts as an essential trace element in living organisms and plays an important role in maintaining life activities. It can participate in a variety of physiological activities, such as oxygen binding and transportation, antibody generation and gene regulation [3]. However, the concentration of Fe3+ in living organisms requiring suitable level, excessive or insufficient amounts of Fe3+ can cause some diseases, such as decreased resistance, cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases, anemia and cancer [4–6]. According to the “domestic drinking water quality standard” that was established in 2007, the maximum permitted amount in drinking water was 0.3 mg/mL [7]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a sensitive, convenient and low-cost method for the detection of Fe3+ in the fields of environmental protection and biological health.

Traditional techniques, including inductively coupled plasma-mass (ICP) spectrometry, colorimetry, atomic absorption spectrometry and electrochemical-based techniques have been designed for tracing Fe3+ with fast and sensitive properties [8–15]. However, several limitations still exist in the above methods, such as the requirement of expensive measuring instruments, skilled analysts, tedious sample pretreatment and harsh testing conditions, which are not suitable for actual measurement. Compared with the above traditional methods, fluorescent analytical methods have gained increasing attention owing to their outstanding advantages, such as simple operation, fast detection speed and affordability and visual detection. At present, a series of fluorescent materials, including organic dyes, carbon dots, quantum dots and upconversion nanoparticles, have been designed for the visual discrimination of Fe3+ via a fluorescence quenching strategy [16–20]. However, complicated synthetic routes and the harsh reaction conditions often limit their further application.

Fluorescent polymer dots (PDs) have attracted increasing attention in the fields of chemical sensors and optoelectronics due to their abundant functional groups, good water dispersion, excellent optical and chemical stability and nontoxic properties, which make them suitable for ion detection [21–25]. The main processes for the preparation of PDs are dehydration, condensation, entanglement and cross-linking, which result in the formation of fluorescent aggregated polymers with nanostructures. However, the major precursors used for PDs preparation are polydopamine, branched polyethyleneimine and poly (ether amide). Therefore, the utilization of novel polymer precursors, especially those based on renewable natural polymers is highly important for designing fluorescent sensors. Recently, fluorescent PDs have been prepared using carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) as the precursor for the detection of tetracyclines, lysine enantiomers and glucose [26–28], which offers theoretical and technical support for the design of novel PDs. However, the fabrication of fluorescent sensors based on CMC with simple preparation, mild reaction conditions and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions still needs to be further studied.

Herein, fluorescent polymer dots (T-PDs) were synthesized by using carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and tartaric acid as precursors, and the T-PDs could be easily obtained via a facile one-step method with magnetic agitation under mild conditions. Moreover, the designed T-PDs can be utilized as a fluorescent platform for tracing Fe3+ through the fluorescence “ON”-“OFF” strategy via internal filter effect (IFE) mechanism. Additionally, the fluorescence of the T-PDs/Fe3+ can be partially recovered by treatment with L-cysteine and logic gate operation can be realized, which has great potential for application in the design of smart devices.

Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, viscosity: 600–3000 MPa.s) and artaric acid were obtained from Macklin Reagent. N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent. Other substances including inorganic salts and amino acids were purchased from the Kermel Chemical Reagents Development Centre (Tianjin, China). All the solvents were purchased from commercial sources and deionized water (18.25 MΩ) was used during the experiments.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a Spectrum400 Spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The fluorescence (FL) spectra were captured by a LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) at a wavelength of 390 nm. The fluorescence lifetime was recorded by an FLS980 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were recorded by a Shimazu UV-2550 PC spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded by a FEI TECNAI G2 transmission electron microscope (Netherlands). Raman spectra were obtained by a laser confocal Micro Raman spectrometer (DXR2). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed with a XRX-6100 (Shimadzu). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with a monochromatic X-ray source (Al Kaline, 1486.6 eV). All the above experiments were conducted at least three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of tartaric acid-functionalized polymer dots (T-PDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The T-PDs were synthesized through a facile one-step method by using tartaric acid (TA) and adipic dihydrazide-substituted carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC-NH2), which was similar to our previous work [26–28]. Briefly, 10 mL of CMC-NH2 solution (0.5 mg/mL) was added to a 50 mL round-bottom flask, and 100 mg of tartaric acid was added to the above solution. A transparent solution was obtained after treatment with ultrasound for 25 min, after which 2.8 mmol of EDC and 1.4 mmol of NHS were added. The hybrid solution was magnetically stirred at 50 °C for 24 h, and purified by centrifugation at 1000 rpm for 10 min. The pale-yellow T-PDs powders was obtained after filtering the solution with a 0.22 μm filter membrane, dialyzed with a 1000 MW dialysis bag and dried in a freeze-drying oven for 24 h.

The QY of the T-PDs was calculated by selecting quinine sulfate (Φ = 0.546, refractive index n = 1.33, QY = 54.6 %) as the standard substance at a concentration of 0.1 M, and the calculation equation was as follows similar to our previous work [29]:
QY =
Q R
I /
I R
O
D R
/ O D
n 2
/
n R 2
QR is the fluorescence QY of the standard material, and I refers to the measured integrated emission intensity of the T-PDs. OD is the optical density recorded by UV–vis spectroscopy (less than 0.05), and n stands for the refractive index. The subscript R represents the standard sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analytical method for Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: 3 mL of T-PDs aqueous (0.2 mg/mL, pH = 5) was treated with different amounts of Fe3+ for 30 s, the fluorescence spectra were recorded (λexi = 390 nm), and the maximum intensity at 512 nm was detected for quantitative analysis

Real samples, including tap water and snow water obtained from the library and outdoors, were used to evaluate the practicability of the method. Different amounts of Fe3+ were added to the above solution and treated for 30 s, and the FL spectra were monitored at λexi = 390 nm (slit width: 5 nm).

The T-PDs were synthesized via a facile one-step method under mild conditions by using TA and CMC-NH2 as precursors, and the synthetic process is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The obtained T-PDs solution displayed a transparent orange-yellow color and excellent water solubility after dialysis. As shown in Fig. 1 , a spherical structure without any obvious aggregation was observed by TEM image, and the average diameter of the T-PDs was calculated to be 2.5 ± 1.2 nm. The formation of T-PDs was speculated that the primary and secondary amines in CMC-NH2 can react with the –COOH group on tartaric acid, thus forming the –CO-NH- and C = N bonds. The above structures began to fold, shrink and self-assemble, and formed a near-spherical structure of the T-PDs in solution [27,28].

Moreover, FT-IR and XPS analysis were utilized to investigate the structural composition and functional groups of the T-PDs. As shown in Fig. S3, the absorption peak centered at 3420 cm−1 refers to the overlap of the N-H and O-H stretching vibrations. The peak at 2960 cm−1 stands for the stretching vibrations of C-H for –CH3 and –CH2-. The peaks at 1650 and 1564 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C = O and C = N, respectively. The typical peak at 1258 cm−1 represents the C-N stretching vibration, and the peak at 1073 cm−1 represents the C-O-C stretching vibration. The absorption band at 1705 cm−1 represents the bending vibration of the N-H for –CO-NH–, which indicated that dehydration and condensation reactions occurred after the formation of T-PDs [32]. The XPS spectra in Fig. 2 a indicate that three elements for C1s (285.5 eV), N1s (400.0 eV), and O1s (531.3 eV) are present in the T-PDs, with the percentages of 59.39 %, 15.59 %, and 25.02 %, respectively. The high-resolution of C1s spectrum in Fig. 2b displays four peaks at 284.3 eV, 285.8 eV, 287.0 eV, and 288.1 eV, corresponding to C-C, C-O, C-N, and C = O, respectively. The two peaks in the N1s spectrum at 399.6 and 401.6 eV are ascribed to the C-N and N-H bonds, respectively (Fig. 2c). Meanwhile, the O1s spectrum can be fitted by two peaks at 530.9, and 533.0 eV, corresponding to the C = O and C-O, respectively (Fig. 2d). The XPS results are in agreement with the FT-IR analysis results, indicating that TA units are successfully modified on the surface of the T-PDs.

The optical characteristics of the T-PDs were analyzed by UV–vis absorption and FL spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the absorbance peak located at 352 nm refers to the electronic transition of n-π* for C═O, and the peak centered at 425 nm stands for the electron transition of n-π for C═N bonds [33–35]. The designed T-PDs solution showed an orange-yellow color in the daylight, and exhibited a turquoise color emission under irradiation with a 365 nm UV beam (inset of Fig. 3a). The optimal excitation peak (λem) and maximum emission wavelength of the T-PDs were 390 and 512 nm, respectively. The QY was determined to be 12.5 %, which is suitable for the fabrication of fluorescent sensors and is comparable to that of previously reported polymer dot-based sensing platforms [22–28]. As shown in Fig. 3b, upon changing the excitation wavelength from 350 to 440 nm, the T-PDs display excitation-dependent emission with a slight redshift, which was because of the irregular surface defects and quantum confinement effects [29]. Moreover, the influence of pH on the T-PDs was further studied. As shown in Fig. S4, as the pH increased from 1 to 12, the maximum emission wavelength displayed an obvious redshift of nearly 50 nm, and the FL emission at 512 nm greatly decreased in strongly alkaline or acidic solutions. The pH-responsiveness of the P-TDs was attributed to the surface protonation and deprotonation [21]. Additionally, the photostability of the T-PDs was investigated by recording the FL intensity at 512 nm with prolonged 365 nm UV light irradiation. As shown in Fig. S5, the FL intensity displayed a negligible change after two days exposure, indicating the excellent photostability of the T-PDs. Furthermore, the different concentrations of NaCl had little effect on the FL intensity of the T-PDs, which shows great potential for use in bioimaging (Fig. S6). The above results reveal that the T-PDs act as an ideal platform and can be designed as fluorescence probes for sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ detection based on the T-PDs
ce:section.ce:para: The designed T-PDs can be used as promising fluorescence chemosensors for detecting Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the initial fluorescence of the T-PDs was quenched in the presence of Fe3+, the FL intensity at 512 nm decreased upon increasing the amount of Fe3+, and a nearly 75 % quenching efficiency (F0-F)/F0 was achieved when the amount of Fe3+ increased to 600 μM, where F0 is the initial FL intensity of the T-PDs at 512 nm, and F is the FL intensity of the T-PDs at 512 nm after the addition of Fe3+. Two good linear relationships can be found between the quenching efficiency and the amount of Fe3+ in the ranges of 5–60 μM (R2 = 0.998) and 60–600 μM (R2 = 0.997), indicating that the T-PDs based fluorescent sensor can be utilized for the quantitative analysis of Fe3+ (Fig. 4b and Fig. S7). The detection limit (LOD) for Fe3+ was calculated to be 0.74 μM by the equation: LOD = 3σ/S, where σ represents the standard deviation of the signals (n = 5) and S stands for the slope of the linear calibration plot [34], which satisfies the maximum acceptable levels in drinking water permitted by the “Standards for Drinking Water Quality” and is comparable with other reported works for Fe3+ detection (Table S1). Meanwhile, the quenching constant (Ksv) was determined to be 2.78 × 103 by the Stern-Volmer equation, which indicated a static quenching process (Fig. S8) [36]. As shown in Fig. S9, a relative standard deviation of 5.1 % can be achieved for Fe3+ detection, thus demonstrating the excellent reproducibility of the T-PDs based fluorescent sensor. The above findings declare that the as-prepared T-PDs can be used for tracing Fe3+ with feasibility and accurate quantification.

In addition, the influence of other interfering substances including cations, anions and small molecules on the fluorescence quenching effects was also studied. As shown in Figs. S10-S12, compared with other interfering substances, only Fe3+ can significantly quenched the FL of the T-PDs. In addition, in the presence of other metal ions together with Fe3+, Fe3+ can still quench the FL emission of the T-PDs, and the influence of other metal ions can be ignored (Fig. S13). Focusing on the above results, this probe displays excellent selectivity toward Fe3+ detection.

Interestingly, the FL emission of the quenched system can partially return to its original level upon the treatment with L-cysteine, and an FL switch with the “ON-OFF-ON” property can be realized. As shown in Fig. S14a, the FL intensity at 512 nm gradually increased with increasing amounts of L-cysteine, and the recovery efficiency of the FL intensity for T-PDs + Fe3+ system reached approximately 77.7 %. The linear equation between the FL value and L-cysteine content was F = 4030.078C-3755.518 (R2 = 0.994) for L-cysteine concentrations ranging from 3 to 8 mM (Fig. S14b). In comparison with other amino acids, the FL intensity recovery for the quenched system could only be achieved by the treatment of L-cysteine (Fig. S15). The above findings revealed that the T-PDs + Fe3+ system can also be ued for quantitative analyses of L-cysteine.

Furthermore, the mechanism of the Fe3+ detection ability of the T-PDs based fluorescent sensor was further studied. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence lifetime of the T-PDs was determined to be 5.20 ns, and it remained nearly unchanged with the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ (5.13 ns for 500 μM, 5.02 ns for 1 mM), thus confirming a static quenching process occurred and the results were consistent with the quenching constant (Ksv) value. Moreover, the radiative (Vr) and non-radiative (Vnr) decay rates were calculated based on the following equations [37]:

τ =
1
V r
+
V
nr
; Q Y =
V r
V r
+
V
nr
, where τ and QY are the fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield of the T-PDs. The Vr and Vnr rates for pure T-PDs were determined to be 2.404 × 104 s−1, and 1.683 × 106 s−1, respectively. However, the values of Vr and Vnr rates for T-PDS remained nearly unchanged (Vr: 2.490 × 104 s−1, Vnr: 1.743 × 106 s−1) even after the treatment with Fe3+ for 1 mM. In addition, Fig. 5b shows a partial overlap region between the UV–vis spectrum of Fe3+ and the excitation spectrum of the T-PDs, and no additional peaks can be observed for the T-PDs/Fe3+ mixture in the UV–vis spectra compared with those of the pure T-PDs and Fe3+ (Fig. S16), which indicated that no new complexes were formed, and the FL was quenched through the internal filtration effect (IFE) [38]. Moreover, the zeta potentials of the T-PDs and T-PDs + Fe3+ were −176 mV and −97.7 mV, respectively, demonstrating that Fe3+ could be absorbed on the surface of the T-PDs (Fig. S17). Meanwhile, the TEM image in Fig. S18a indicated that Fe3+ could cause obvious aggregation of the T-PDs. Herein, the mechanism for fluorescence quenching was that Fe3+ could be coordinated with the –OH and –NH2 groups on the surface of the T-PDs, and the electrons of the T-PDs would transfer from the excited state to the unfilled orbital of Fe3+, which could lead to non-radiative electron/hole recombination, thus quenching the FL of the T-PDs [26,29].

Interestingly, the FL of the quenched system (Fe3++T-PDs) could be partially recovered by the addition of L-cysteine. As shown in Fig. S19, upon the treatment of the quenched system (T-PDs + Fe3+) with L-cysteine, there was a decrease in the overlap between the UV–vis spectrum of the Fe3++T-PDs + L-cysteine and the excitation spectrum of the T-PDs, which might be ascribed to the stronger binding ability between the sulfhydryl (−SH) unit of L-cysteine and Fe3+. Furthermore, the zeta potential of the T-PDs + Fe3+ + L-cysteine decreased to −133.8 mV (Fig. S17), which indicated that the L-cysteine can capture Fe3+ form the surface of the T-PDs. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S18b, a re-dispersion of the T-PDs could be observed for T-PDs + Fe3+ + L-cysteine system, which also revealed the shows a higher affinity for Fe3+ for L-cysteine. All above results demonstrated that the −SH unit of L-cysteine shows stronger binding ability for Fe3+ compared with T-PDs, it inhibited the Fe3+ coordinated on the surface of the T-PDs which lead to the aggregation for the T-PDs, thus resulting partial recovery of the FL of the quenched system [29].

It was of great value for elucidating the reliability and practicality of T-PDs based fluorescent sensors. Herein, the above sensor was used for detecting Fe3+ in tap water and snow water samples. As shown in Table S2 and S3, the RSD values ranged from 3.59 % to 4.06 % and the recoveries ranged from 99.48 % to 102.10 % for tap water. The RSD values ranged from 3.55 % to 4.16 % and the recoveries ranged from 98.95 % to 103.53 % for snow water. Overall, this proposed fluorescent sensor was suitable for detecting Fe3+ in real samples with reliability and practicality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fabrication of configurable logic gate
ce:section.ce:para: The construction of configurable logic gate is highly important for designing smart instruments for the application of information storage with multiple write-read-erasable forms. Herein, an “INHIBIT” logic gate operation can be realized for T-PDs. The T-PDs with FL emission was defined as the initial state, and the output signal was defined as follows: the FL in the “ON” state was “1”, and the FL in the “OFF” state was “0”. Additionally, Fe3+ and L-cysteine acted as the two inputs, and the absence or presence of either of them was defined as “0” or “1”. As shown in Fig. 6 , in the absence of Fe3+ and L-cysteine (input: 0/0), obvious FL emission was observed for the T-PDs, and the signal of the output was “1”. With the addition of Fe3+ (input: 1/0), the FL of the T-PDs could be quenched by the IFE process, and the signal of the output was “0”. With only the addition of L-cysteine (input: 0/1), the FL emission of the T-PDs remained unchanged, and the output was “1”. Interestingly, in the presence of both Fe3+ and L-cysteine (input: 1/1), the FL of the system could be partially recovered, and the output was “1”.

In summary, T-PDs were synthesized through a facile and mild technique by using TA and CMC-NH2 as precursors. The T-PDs display an average size, excellent water solubility and turquoise colored FL emission with a QY of 12.5 %. The FL of the T-PDs can be quenched by Fe3+ via the mechanism of IFE, and a T-PDs-based sensing platform was developed. The as-prepared fluorescent sensor displays high sensitivity and selectivity, a low LOD and a wide linear concentration range for Fe3+ detection. Moreover, it can also be utilized for the detection of Fe3+ in real samples with comparable reliability and practicality. Furthermore, FL with an “ON-OFF-ON” switch can be realized by the treatment with Fe3+ and L-cysteine, and an “INHIBIT” logic gate operation can be designed, which may pave the way for developing smart fluorescent devices in the field of information computing and anti-fake information encryption.

Zhonghao Li: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Dan Li: Formal analysis, Investigation, Supervision. Wenting Du: Data curation, Supervision. Yinuo Du: Writing – review & editing. Guang Yang: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support from Heilongjiang Provincial Undergraduate Innovative and Entrepreneurial Training Program (202310225466) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572021BU05).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.115862.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heavy metals and human health: Possible exposure pathways and the competition for protein binding sites
Molecules
